title: Operating Systems: Three Easy Pieces
author: Remzi H. Arpaci-Dusseau and Andrea C. Arpaci-Dusseau
subtitle: Virtualization, Concurrency, and Persistence — the Three Pillars of Operating Systems
description: One of the most readable and widely admired OS textbooks ever written. Remzi and Andrea Arpaci-Dusseau, professors at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, break the subject into three intuitive pieces — virtualization (CPU and memory), concurrency (threads and synchronization), and persistence (file systems and storage) — and explain each with clear prose, real code examples, and clever analogies. The book is freely available online and has become the de-facto modern replacement for older OS textbooks.
keyIdeas: Virtualization creates the illusion that each process owns the entire CPU and memory
Limited direct execution (LDE) lets the OS run processes efficiently while retaining control via timer interrupts
Scheduling policies (FIFO, SJF, MLFQ, CFS) balance fairness, turnaround time, and response time
Memory virtualization uses base-and-bounds, segmentation, paging, and TLBs to give each process its own address space
Concurrency introduces threads, locks, semaphores, condition variables, and the challenge of synchronization
Persistence requires file systems to manage data on disk with crash consistency guarantees
Journaling (write-ahead logging) ensures file system consistency after crashes
SSDs change the performance model of storage — no mechanical seeks, but erase-before-write and wear leveling
keyTakeaways: The OS is a resource manager that virtualizes hardware to provide clean abstractions to user programs
Virtualization is achieved through a combination of hardware support and OS policies (mechanism vs. policy separation)
Scheduling is about trade-offs — no single policy is optimal for all workloads
Paging with TLBs is the dominant approach to memory virtualization, balancing flexibility and performance
Concurrency bugs (race conditions, deadlocks, atomicity violations) are hard to find and harder to fix
Locks, semaphores, and condition variables are powerful synchronization primitives that must be used with care
File systems must survive crashes — journaling and copy-on-write are the main approaches
SSDs are fundamentally different from disks; OSes must adapt their I/O schedulers and data layouts accordingly
whoShouldRead: Undergraduate CS students taking their first OS course
Self-taught programmers who never formally studied operating systems
Software engineers who want to understand what happens below their code at the OS level
Anyone preparing for systems programming interviews (scheduling, memory, concurrency)
